request_metadata.title: Oxygen management for adults in acute care facilities

primary: All Nursing staff responsible for patients in acute care facilities
All Medical staff responsible for patients in acute care facilities
Midwifery staff responsible for patients in acute care facilities
Physiotherapy staff working with patients who may have oxygen in situ
target_audience.setting: Acute care facilities (mandatory training); also useful for primary health sector (e.g., HITH, CDCRS)
target_audience.scope: All Nursing, Midwifery, and Medical staff (and some Physiotherapy staff) employed by NSW Health

learning_need_context.learning_need_description: Not to be confused with the MyHealthLearning module: Oxygen therapy in acutely ill adults (Q stream spaced learning, currently unavailable). This proposed module would provide current, evidence-based education to address widespread misunderstandings about oxygen management.
priority_rationale: Almost all acutely unwell patients require supplemental oxygen at some point
Oxygen is like a drug: risks associated with administration; mismanagement can lead to avoidable harm or death
Common mismanagement due to lack of understanding and false assumption that oxygen is safe
Supported by Subject Matter Experts across respiratory and patient deterioration roles
Complacency around oxygen management is real; education often not prioritised unless post-adverse event
Need for easily accessible, evidence-based, consistent messaging across LHDs/facilities
MyHealthLearning platform is familiar to clinicians; completion would be trackable by educators/managers
barriers_to_current_performance: No formal requirement for oxygen management training
No consistent method of education delivery
No consistent, formal oxygen prescription across LHDs
Complacency and false conception that oxygen is a safe drug
Misunderstanding particularly regarding use in hypercapnia
Incidents of delayed escalation due to overreliance on SpO2 monitoring ('oxygen creep')

knowledge_and_skills: Understand common terms: hypoxia, hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, hyperoxia
Understand different oxygen delivery devices, minimum/maximum flow rates, and why misuse is unsafe
Know when to reference L/min vs FiO2 for different devices
Understand rationale for oxygen prescribing and interpretation (including maximum dose before escalation)
Ensure consistency across NSW, especially for rotating Junior Medical Officers
Understand rationale for altering Between the Flags (BTF) calling criteria, including prescribing upper limits for weaning
Recognise indication for oxygen supplementation: only for hypoxia
Understand that oxygen does not treat breathlessness; know alternative strategies
Considerations for entraining oxygen through home CPAP/BiPAP devices (optional/advanced)
practice_improvement: Change culture and management of oxygen where past recommendations have failed
Provide trackable, ideally mandatory resource for nurses and junior doctors
Reduce avoidable harm and adverse outcomes:
- Reduced need for closer observation, HDU/ICU transfer
- Reduced hospital length of stay and hospital-acquired complications
- Reduced patient/family distress, financial burden, system costs
- Prevention of temporary/permanent disability or death
patient_benefits: Upskilled workforce improves safety and quality of clinical practice
Better management of deteriorating patients requiring oxygen
Prevention of avoidable harm and suffering from oxygen mismanagement
local_performance_agreement_alignment: - Pressure-related injury from device mismanagement (prolonged supplemental oxygen)
- Fall-related injuries secondary to hypoxia/hypercapnia/altered consciousness
- Infection risk from prolonged length of stay
- Respiratory complications: respiratory compromise, failure, pneumonia associated with oxygen administration

outcomes.goal: Safe care is delivered across all settings
outcomes.objective: 2.1 Deliver safe, high quality reliable care for patients in hospital and other settings
outcomes.contribution: Evidence-based, consistent education aligned with TSANZ Position Statement; reduces unwarranted clinical variation, improves safety, reduces adverse incidents

outcomes.goal: Our staff are engaged and well supported
outcomes.objective: 4.4 Equip our people with the skills and capabilities to be an agile, responsive workforce
outcomes.contribution: Accessible, evidence-based resource addressing different learning styles; addresses critical but misunderstood intervention

reference_materials.title: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) Position Statement on Acute Oxygen Use in Adults: 'Swimming between the flags' (2022)
reference_materials.notes: TSANZ may have additional resources available on request

supporting_frameworks: Ministry of Health: awareness of formal oxygen requirements under SDPR; buy-in for mandatory module status

subject_matter_experts.name: Mary Roberts
subject_matter_experts.expertise: Nursing input; video resource on breathlessness management

additional_notes: Requester has not yet discussed this application with listed clinicians
Module should avoid confusion between facilities, especially for rotating JMOs
Content should be accessible, evidence-based, and support consistent messaging statewide
